[[RunningHead]]

The Development of an Institute for the Caring Professions

Alan Harrow , Murray Leishman and Judith Brearley

Judith Brearley

56 Albany Street
Edinburgh EH1 3QR

Judith Brearley  

This paper was originally given in Durham in March 1988 at a conference of the Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the NHS, the Scottish Association of Analytical Psychotherapists and the Northern Association for Analytic Psychotherapy. It explores the processes of development of a single organisation, the Scottish Institute of Human Relations, since its inception twenty years ago. The three authors have worked together in different roles throughout this period, and they write from their separate subjective experiences as well as indicating areas of joint concern.

First, an overview of the history of the Institute is offered by the Director, with comment on some of the achievements and vicissitudes and the learning which resulted. Secondly, the Institute's responses to a particular group of users, the clergy, is described and illustrated. Finally, the change process is discussed, both theoretically and with reference to various aspects of the Institute's life, especially relationships within and between internal groupings and with the outside world.

Letter to the Editor

Isobel Hunter-Brown

Isobel Hunter-Brown

Isobel Hunter-Brown   Associate Member, British Institute of Psycho-Analysis formerly Consultant Psychiatrist/Psychotherapist Student Health Service, University of Leicester

From Dr Isobel H Hunter-Brown

Dear Editor

In your ‘APP Conference Summary’ report (Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, 4 pp. 77-91), in the section about Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the British Health Service, it is stated that ‘There are only about sixty consultant psychotherapy posts, all concentrated in London’ (page 79, top). May I comment that there have been NHS Consultant Psychotherapy posts in Nottingham, in Derby and in Leicester for a considerable number of years. I suggest you might also check whether or not there are NHS Consultant Psychotherapy posts elsewhere too —? Manchester, Leeds, Scotland (Edinburgh and Glasgow) and ?? Newcastle, Birmingham, for example.

I think a recent psychotherapy post (but possibly non-consultant) in Nottingham was advertised to psychoanalysts in the Newsletter

The statement referred to must, of course, have a regrettable impact on the individuals who hold the posts, whom, presumably, the APP wishes to influence, and I trust you will appreciate my pointing out the problem.

197

[[RunningHead]]

Reply from Brian Martindale [Compiler Of The APP Conference Report]

We thank Dr Hunter-Brown for drawing our attention to the important distortion of facts that regrettably got into the summary I made of the paper that Dr Temple gave at the APP European Conference.

Extrapolating from statistical information made available to me by the Royal College of Psychiatrists, it seems that there are in fact 124 Consultant Psychotherapy posts in England, thirteen in Scotland, two in Wales and one in Northern Ireland. Of the 124 posts in England, sixty-seven are in London or Greater London and the majority of these are part-time.

197

Disavowal and Assessment for Psychotherapy

Victor Sedlak PHD

Victor Sedlak

51 Woodsome Road
London NW5 1SA

Victor Sedlak  

The concept of disavowal is described and its part in the pathology of a certain group of patients is discussed. Particular emphasis is placed upon the difficulties posed by the pervasive use of this defence mechanism during the assessment of such patients. Clinical vignettes from assessment interviews are presented. The longer-term psychotherapeutic management of these patients is discussed and this is illustrated by clinical material.

responseSet.title: A Quality Assurance Programme for Psychotherapy—The Finnish Experience
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Quality Assurance Programme for Psychotherapy—The Finnish Experience

Kari PylkkäNen

Kari PylkkäNen

Siltasaarenkatu 18 A, PB 223
SF-00531 Helsinki 53 Finland.

Kari PylkkäNen   Psychoanalyst and Director of Mental Health at The National Board of Health in Finland. He has been chairing the Finnish Task Force for psychotherapy.

In many respects the status of psychotherapy in public health-care systems is similar to that of alternative medicine. The roots of training are outside universities. Legitimation and public funding have been non-existent or insufficient as compared with other fields of health care. Also a great deal of mysticism is attached to both. It is therefore relevant to study the role of psychotherapy from the frame of reference given by research into alternative health care.

Formal health care is characterised by (1) legalised status, (2) access to public funding and (3) integration in the formal medical curriculum. On the other hand there are several treatment philosophies which remain as outsiders from the point of view of formal health care. Informal health care approaches are characterised by (1) lack of legalised status, (2) no access to public funding and (3) no integration in the medical curriculum. Alternative medicine is a term usually attached to forms of informal health care like acupuncture, Spa-culture etc.

Psychotherapy in the public health sector lies somewhere between formal and informal health care. While it has no legalised status, anyone may call himself ‘psychotherapist’. Its definition remains obscure and its public accountability poor.

In this paper I have tried to show how one country — Finland — has tried to grapple with this problem as it attempted to set up psychotherapy services whose quality could be properly monitored — ‘Quality Assurance’ — allowing for public accountability. Now that psychotherapy is well established as a valuable treatment in the field of mental health it is possible to consider future expansion as a cost-effective way to improve the general mental health of the country.

responseSet.title: App Conference Summary: Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Services in Europe
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

App Conference Summary

Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Services in Europe

Introduction

In November 1988, the APP initiated and hosted a three-day conference on “Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Services in Europe”. Some 160 participants attended from fifteen countries and some of the papers appear in this number in full. After considerable thought it was decided to summarise for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy the nine formal presentations. The lengthy discussions that followed the papers would be impossible to précis, but a number of points stood out.

Firstly, in a number of countries, psychoanalytic psychotherapy is emerging as a distinct profession and therapy in its own right, complementary to psychoanalysis but beginning to evolve its own organisational frameworks and high standards for training. This seems to stem from (a) an increasing ability to face the reality that there will never be enough psychoanalysts to offer psychoanalysis to the large number of patients suffering from neurotic and non-psychotic character disorders, (b) a preparedness to try and meet the public need in as efficient and quality-conscious way as possible. This has involved (c) a less paranoid and more collaborative relationship with third-party agencies, e.g. government and health administration, insurance companies, and psychiatric colleagues.

Secondly, British participants must have been acutely aware of how far behind some other countries we are, as psychoanalytic psychotherapists, in taking the initiative in these matters. This contrasts starkly with our history, when, particularly following the World Wars, pioneers founded such institutions as the Tavistock Institute, Portman Clinic and Cassel Hospital.

The third issue is the question of how we can build on the knowledge that we possess and of the additional knowledge that we have gained from our overseas colleagues. It was clear that several countries, because of a shortage of time at the Conference, had a lot of experience that they were unable to share in full. One, partial solution was expressed by the hope that there could be increasing exchange of seminal scientific papers on psychoanalytic psychotherapy between European countries.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy as One form of Rehabilitation Provided by the Finnish Social Insurance Institution

Kalevi Seppälä and Heikki Jämsén

Heikki Jämsén

Dr Kalevi Seppälä
00381 Helsinki, Finland

Heikki Jämsén  

One of the several methods whereby patients in Finland may receive subsidised psychotherapy is as a component of rehabilitation, in order to improve a person's working and functional capacity. The Finnish Social Insurance Institution has carefully documented and monitored the effects of this intervention on patients. It is well known that it is difficult to measure the success of psychotherapy, but it appears that the results of psychotherapy (mainly psychoanalytic) are good, both according to the patients’ own assessment and as indicated by the changes in the their functional capacities. More than 80 per cent benefited and about one third became free of symptoms of functional deficiency. The neurotic group appeared to have derived the greatest benefit from therapy.

Some Thoughts about the Function of Gaze-Avoidance in Early Infancy: a Mother-Baby Observation

Luigi Caparrotta

Luigi Caparrotta

17 St Gabriels Road
London, NW2 4DS

Luigi Caparrotta  

This paper examines the role of gaze-avoidance in early infancy. After a brief review of the literature on the subject, there is a discussion based on an infant observation material. The importance of a meaningful eye-to-eye contact between mother and baby is stressed. The author takes the view that gaze aversion could be seen as a response of the infant to maintain his sense of self when threatened with overwhelming anxiety.

Evaluating the Performance of Departments of Psychotherapy

Peter Fonagy PHD DIPPSYCH and Anna Higgitt BSC MB BS MRCPSYCH MD

Anna Higgitt

Gower Street
London WC1E 6BT

Praed Street
London W2 1NY

Anna Higgitt  

Conceptual and practical issues which surround attempts at assessing the efficiency of psychotherapy departments are surveyed and the advantages and disadvantages associated with performance indicators reviewed. The issues of assessing efficiency in general and the selection of appropriate indicators at both national and local levels are discussed. It is concluded that it is possible to find indicators which reflect the value of a psychotherapeutic service to the community within which it is based, and that the identification of a generally agreed set of such parameters is a matter of priority.

responseSet.title: When it is Not the Patient Who Pays
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When it is Not the Patient Who Pays

Leo Nobel

Leo Nobel

Goeman Borgesiuslaan 12
1181 XZ Amstelveen
The Netherlands

Leo Nobel  

This paper looks at the question of third-party payments in psychotherapy and psychoanalysis. This is of particular relevance in Holland, where the Dutch government makes substantial payments for psychoanalytic treatment via a state insurance system. The literature concerning third-party payments is reviewed. Freud himself was inconsistent about this matter and cannot therefore be relied on to give a clear guideline. The majority of writers are heavily in favour of ‘personal sacrifice’ and show a strong bias, especially against insurance payments for fees. These arguments are examined in detail and the two main conclusions are explored, namely that (1) certain unconscious conflicts cannot be mobilised where there is external financing, and (2) that the absence of personal sacrifice engenders too much wish-fulfilment. Both these arguments are rejected as being biased and are found not to stand up to detailed scrutiny.

The effects that different types of payment have on the development of the therapeutic situation, and the different types of transference and countertransference problems that each engenders, are explored. There is some speculation as to the reason for the persistence of this view if it is so patently false. Finally there is some consideration of the positive effects third-party payments can have on psychoanalytic practice.

[[RunningHead]]

The Internal Family and the Facts of Life

Deborah Steiner

Deborah Steiner

28 Park Drive
London NW11 7SP

Deborah Steiner  

Primitive defensive mechanisms innvolving splitting and projections are an essential process in the development of the infant. However, if these persist into adult life unmitigated by more depressive processes involving introjection and awareness of secure good objects they are shown to be inadequate to the task of coping with life crises, based as they are on the denial and distortion of reality. In this paper, I describe two patients who were seen in very different treatment settings and in whom such mechanisms were predominant. These began to fail when the patients were faced with difficult external events. These caused feelings of panic about impending mental disintegration. I then show how these patients sought to re-establish their primitive defensive structures.

Lines of Advance in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy**

Jonathan Pedder MA MRCP FRCPSYCH

Jonathan Pedder

26 Alwyne Road
London NI 2HN

Jonathan Pedder  

Freud's 1919 paper is taken as a starting-point to review how far we have advanced towards fulfilment of his prophetic remarks about the need to develop psychoanalytic psychotherapy for the masses. A glance is taken back to some of the circumstances and characters involved in Budapest where the paper was given at the 1918 congress, where there was much talk about the need to do something about the war neuroses. Similar pressures led to the founding of the Tavistock Clinic and Cassel Hospital in the UK, long before the start of the NHS. The pressures of both world wars advanced the applications of psychoanalysis; the next wave of pressures may come from NHS changes and entry into Europe. The history of psychoanalytic psychotherapy in the UK is also considered via looking briefly at the history and development of psychoanalytic publications, of the Tavistock Clinic, of psychoanalytic training, of child psychotherapy and the emergence of adult (non-medical) psychotherapy. There have always been tensions between exponents of the pure gold of analysis and the needs of its wider applications. More flexible arrangements and boundaries are necessary if we are to spread psychoanalysis beyond London and to fulfil Freud's own hopes about the large-scale application of psychoanalytic therapy.

[[RunningHead]]

Supervision: A Didactic or Mutative Situation

Imre Szecsödy

Imre Szecsödy

Karlavägen 27
114 31 Stockholm Sweden

Imre Szecsödy  

This paper discusses the nature of learning in the supervision of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. It considers the complexities of the relationship between therapist and supervisor, especially in the context of a training where the supervising relationship occurs as part of complex relationships to the parent institution and is subject to potentially conflicting pressures. It is with this background that the author examines methods of supervision in the literature, noting that there have been few systematic studies and that there is certainly no standard or generally accepted method. The author discusses the nature of learning, especially from a psychoanalytic viewpoint, and then considers his own systematic study of supervision, which included the detailed study of transcripts of supervision sessions. This enables exploration of the interactive process between supervisor and therapist and allows for some general consideration of the types of interaction which allow for mutative change in the therapist: ‘transmutative situation’. Some examples are given and finally conclusions are drawn about the essential and desirable prerequisites for learning and teaching in supervision.

The purpose of this paper is to convey more widely the existence of this kind of specialist group in the NHS. We also wish to show some of the advantages and disadvantages in setting up such a group to other professionals who may be considering the possibility of starting an incest-survivors group. The paper describes the group which ran at the Psychiatric Day Hospital at the Whittington Hospital for forty weeks: how we adopted the group structure; some of the problems we faced; and some of the emotional issues generated in therapists and patients. responseSet.title: Countertransference as a Complex Tool for Understanding the Patient in Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference as a Complex Tool for Understanding the Patient in Psychotherapy**

Joscelyn Richards

Joscelyn Richards

Harlesden Road
London NW10 3RY

Joscelyn Richards  

This paper shows some of the complexities of the analytic relationship and processes in psychotherapy by focusing on the concept of countertransference. The development of the related concept of transference is briefly outlined and the history of the concept of countertransference and its place in the practice of psychoanalysis is presented. A clinical example is provided to illustrate some of the complexity of the issues that arise when the countertransference is used as a tool to understand the patient. Attention is drawn to the importance of sustaining the countertransference response until the transference aspects and the patient's unconscious communications have been recognised and understood. It is only at this point that an interpretation meaningful to the patient can be made.

Development and Psychopathology. Studies in Psychoanalytic Psychiatry by Clifford Yorke, Stanley Wiseberg & Thomas Freeman. New Haven & New York: Yale Univ. Press. Pp. 243 £22.50.

Brian Martindale

Brian Martindale

Brian Martindale  

This book is the work of three psychiatrists who have worked and researched at the Anna Freud Centre and who also have extensive experience within the National Health Service. Two of the authors are also eminent psychoanalysts. Their aim is to convey a psychoanalytic theory of a arange of disorders commonly encountered by psychiatrists, ranging from anxiety states through the various neuroses and personality disorders, to the common psychoses.

Throughout, they mainly take a developmental perspective, showing where possible the infant and childhood antecedents that have left the subsequent adolescent and adult vulnerable to neurotic and/or psychotic disturbances. They devote a considerable amount of space to spelling out their views on the psychodynamic reasons why a disturbance has both a particular form and content, and to discussing the internal and external circumstances that may have led to its appearance at a particular time.

Half of the book is on psychotic disorders. Many will find valuable the presentation of the differing emphases in the various psychoanalytic theories of schizophrenia, starting with Freud and the Schreber case, and moving on to the differing ideas of Katan, Bak and Klein. The question of which are primary and which are secondary phenomena in psychoses remains intriguing and disputed. An account is given of the fundamental difference in opinion on the fate of the ego in schizophrenic and paranoid psychosis between Kleinians and classical analysts.

The authors are particularly good at spelling out classification issues, whether they are discussing different categories within the anxiety states, the neuroses, character disorders or psychoses, or the essential differences between these [and other] major groupings. Psychoanalysts have, for various reasons, tended to disparage classification and to overlook how helpful it can be for accurate communication and discussion, and for the development of metapsychologies, when phenomena are well defined and classified. An example of this is the authors’ observation that clinical phenomena occurring in manic states following object-loss are different from those following object frustrations and disappointments. This leads to differing metapsychological formulations.

[[RunningHead]]

Borderline and Narcissistic Patients in Therapy edited by Nonna Slavinska-Holy. New York: Int. Univ. Press.

John Lawrence

John Lawrence

John Lawrence  

This collection of papers, all in some way clinically oriented, although uneven in quality and depth, is often useful and interesting. The reader is given ample chance to explore contributions written from the self-psychology perspective and also from psychoanalytical humanism. I was interested by how often the self-psychology contributors, in particular, appear to recoil from trying to help patients face destructive aspects of their psychotic processes. However, much case material is offered, allowing the reader to understand something of why some of the authors considerably emphasise the positive in their approach to patients.

A range of psychotherapy methods is described and presented. Hence the book seems to be posing questions about what is curative or mutative in treatment: is it the method used or the kinds of understanding potentially available in the therapist? Although the contributors argue for their own favoured modes of practice, there was no discussion that I could find about the relationship between treatment methods (individual, group, combined, etc.) and possible models of understanding. Amongst the types of treatment included are individual and group therapy (sometimes combined), marital therapy, milieu therapy, drama therapy, and psychopharmacological therapy. Some chapters describe in-patient settings where several methods are offered to patients in combination. Readers working within institutions, particularly in-patient settings, may find some interesting points of contact with their own practice.

One of the best chapters in the collection is by Brandchaft & Stolorow, entitled “The Difficult Patient”. They write fervently from the standpoint of self-psychology and from their belief that ideas stemming from Heinz Kohut's work have brought to hand a new way of understanding clinical material. They include within their interesting and carefully-argued paper a vivid and concise clinical illustration. This I thought enabled the reader to test his or her own point of view against a possibly rather different kind of outlook for which the writers argue passionately. They begin by strongly advocating the idea that the analyst must strive for an observational stance which “places primary emphasis on understanding the impact of the analyst from within the patient's subjective frame of reference.” We might understand this as roughly equivalent to trying to think about how the patient experiences the therapist. There then follows an excellent clinical extract from the Monday session of a narcissistic patient in analysis to illustrate their main points.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Remembering and Repeating in Multiple Personality

Ely Garfinkle MD FRCP(C)

Ely Garfinkle

3814 Northcliffe
Montreal Canada H4A 3L1

Ely Garfinkle  

This paper is a case-history of a student in his early twenties tortured by different personalities of different ages and sexes vying for control of his body. Guilt over an incestuous relationship with his mother is uncovered and understood in the context of his whole life. Confusion between memories of fantasies, memories of sleep dreams, memories of daydreams, and memories of actual memories are described, the elucidation of which lead to clearer thinking and memory, and more tolerance for the previously distorted affect-laden situations of his past. Thus his belief that he was experimented on in an alien spaceship becomes the memory of an operating-room diagnostic experience.

A hypothesis is put forward that the separate personalities in the multiple personality are not simply the products of ego splits, but are at the instant they are formed a ‘doubling’ of the personality that is being copied. Immediately thereafter, splitting mechanisms create differences between the two separate personalities, using protective and introjective mechanisms. Integration of the various personalities, the ultimate goal in treatment, is made difficult by the patient's fear of loss of self through fragmentation, when the self is felt to be in danger of being intruded upon.

These books on psychiatry are included here for the benefit of non-medical psychotherapists who might wish to have a reference book, and because they are part-written by psychoanalysts. The first has a more specialised function and performs this task well. For the non-medical reader there are useful notes on the symptoms and signs of organic brain disease, actions and side-effects of psychotropic drugs, and descriptive psychopathology.

The second book is a major general textbook which replaces the popular UCH Handbook of Psychiatry. The emphasis throughout is on an integration of descriptive, psychological and social aspects of psychiatry, and it manages to achieve a remarkable consistency in this endeavour, given that it is a multi-author textbook.

Based on the method of infant observation developed by Esther Bick, this book

285

represents the distillation of forty years’ experience. Its publication last December was marked by a short conference, in which the contributions of this method of infant observation to social work training were discussed, and links with the developments in empirical psychological research were emphasised. The book makes a timely contribution to the current developments in cross-fertilisation between different fields of research into infant development. Hopefully it will also promote such developments within the psychoanalytic field too.

The book is divided into two parts. The first chapters consider theoretical aspects of infant development and the method of observation. The second and longer part gives clinical material from the observations of eight child psychotherapists (including one set of twins).

The theoretical chapters are well laid out and clearly written. They describe the authors’ psychoanalytic understanding of infant development, and the relevance of infant observation to clinical practice. In the third chapter Michael Rustin discusses the theory of this method and reflects on the research model that could be developed. He refers also the links with and differences from empirical research.

The clinical chapters contain a great deal of fascinating and moving material, which is presented as recorded by the observers, leaving room for the reader's own views and understanding to develop.

This is an important book and a valuable (and inexpensive) addition to any psychotherapist's library.

286

This is a good primer written by a clinical nursing specialist in psychotherapy especially for student nurses, but also for other professionals beginning work in mental-health care, primarily relating to psychiatric establishments. Emphasis is placed on the mechanisms used in communication between mother and baby and their subsequent understanding and use in therapeutic groups. The book's main value is in its simplification of psychoanalytic concepts of early mental mechanisms, such as splitting and projective identification, and how they manifest themselves in groups in a psychiatric setting where boundary issues and clarification of roles are essential to management. The psychodynamic focus is vividly illustrated with clinical examples from the author's experience and linked to their sources in basic psychoanalytic texts.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How can Psychotherapists Influence Psychiatry?

Jonathan Pedder

Jonathan Pedder

London, SE5 8AZ

Jonathan Pedder  

This paper is based on an invited contribution to the first APP conference on psychoanalytic psychotherapy services in Europe. I was asked to address the question “How can psychotherapists influence psychiatry?” within the overall title for the half-day session of “Psychiatry and Psychodynamics: Towards integration or splitting”. As a background to later discussion I began with a historical sketch of how we had arrived at the current situation in the UK with a specialty of psychotherapy, both separate from and yet still within psychiatry. I argued the need for a separate specialty to protect the space needed for psychotherapy in the face of the inevitable polarisations that occur within psychiatry. Some of the tensions between the different points of view are likened to those between masculine and feminine roles. This specialty might eventually emerge into or foster a new profession of psychotherapy but will still need to negotiate its position in relation to psychiatry.

Psychoanalysis and the Political Process

Anton Obholzer

Anton Obholzer

120 Belsize Lane
London NW3 5BA

Anton Obholzer  

The Political Process is Society's mechanism for debating value systems. The term can also be used to describe a debate in a variety of other settings — subsystems of Society — about value systems. This paper is about the contributions that psychoanalysis, both as a theory and in its various applications, can make to the understanding of the process of debate and its setting. It is also about the implications for our conduct that arise from this understanding.

responseSet.title: Different Origins and Meanings of Acute Acting-out in an Inpatient Psychotherapeutic Setting
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Different Origins and Meanings of Acute Acting-out in an Inpatient Psychotherapeutic Setting

Marco Chiesa MD MRCPSYCH

Marco Chiesa

1 Ham Common
Richmond Surrey TW10 7JF.

Marco Chiesa  

In this paper I describe four different sources of acute acting-out behaviour as may be observed in long-term hospitalised patients suffering from severe personality disorders. I hope to demonstrate that acute psychological distress does not happen in a vacuum, but that specific intrapsychic and interpersonal dynamics within the hospital setting are at the root of what appears overtly as nonspecific emotional and behavioural disturbance. I argue that the hospital team's correct insight into the factors underlying acute acting-out is crucial to the implementation of therapeutic interventions aimed to decrease the patient's disturbed state of mind.

responseSet.title: The Aim of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Aim of Psychoanalysis**

John Steiner

John Steiner

120 Belsize Lane
London NW3 5BA

John Steiner  

The changing aims of psychoanalysis are discussed with reference to Money-Kyrle's paper on the subject. The theory of dammed-up libido, the theory of moral conflict and the theory of misrepresentations and perversion of reality which he described, all imply different aims. It is argued that, in addition, the theory of protective identification leads to a radical change in the aim of treatment which can now be formulated in terms of helping the patient regain lost parts of the self.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Becoming Dependent again: The Fears of Some Elderly Persons and their Younger Therapists

Brian Martindale

Brian Martindale

63 Lancaster Road
London W11 1QG

Brian Martindale  

In this paper I shall be discussing only a few of the many aspects of dependency in later life that cause difficulties for some elderly persons and for their younger therapists. I shall be particularly emphasising the fear of dependency in both partners in the therapeutic relationship, and the central role that this fear can play in both bringing the elderly person to the mental health professional for help, and in interfering with the provision of help.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Melanie Klein Today. Volume I Mainly Theory; Volume II Mainly Practice. The Library of Psychoanalysis edited by Elizabeth Bott-Spillius. London: Routledge. £14.95 per vol.

Jon Sklar

Jon Sklar

Jon Sklar  

In 1955, Mrs Klein and her colleagues published a notable volume New Directions in Psychoanalysis. The two books presently reviewed continue the process of reviewing Kleinian theory and technique since Mrs Klein's New Directions. They contain a feast of important and valuable, even historical papers, grouped round certain themes.

In Volume I, the four areas contained are: analysis of psychotic patients, projective identification, thinking, and pathological organisations. Each area is skilfully introduced and connected to the others by the Editor. In the second volume, which is mainly practice in psychoanalysis, the four areas traversed are: developments in technique, clinical papers on adult psychoanalysis, analysis of children, and application in other fields.

In these volumes one will find Segal's “Depression in the Schizophrenic” and “Notes on Symbol Formation”. Also Bion's “Attacks on Linking, Differentiation of the Psychotic from the Non-Psychotic Personalities” and “The Theory of Thinking”; Meltzer's “The Relation of Anal Masturbation to Projective Identification” and his paper on “Terror, Persecution and Dread”; and papers by Joseph, Brenman, Sohn, Rey and Steiner.

Similarly, in the second volume, is Bion's “Notes on Memory and Desire” from 1967, Money-Kyrle's 1956 paper on “Normal Countertransference and Some of Its Deviations” and Segal's 1950 paper on “Some Aspects of the Analysis of a Schizophrenic”. Volume II also develops Joseph's themes in her 1975 paper “The Patient Who Is Difficult To Reach” and her paper, ten years later, on “Transference — the Total Situation”. In the section on the analysis of children, one finds papers by Harris, Bick, O'Shaughnessy, and de Folch. The last four papers on “Applications of Kleinian Ideas in Other Fields of Work” has Jaques’ 1965 paper on “Death and the Mid-Life Crisis”, and Segal's paper on “William Golding”; Menzies-Lyth on “Social Institutions”; and Spillius on “Ceremony”.

Dr Herbert Rosenfeld has three papers in the first volume. These are his 1952 paper on “The Superego Conflict of an Acute Schizophrenic Patient”, his 1971 paper on “Psychotic States” and his other 1971 paper on the theory of “The Life and Death Instincts”. Yet, his important contributions, published in 1987 in “Impasse and Interpretation”, in which he reviews his early directions and in particular the importance of early trauma, and his critique of some aspects of Kleinian technique as he sees it, is missing. The volume does contain papers published as late as 1988. So one is left to ponder on what is accepted as having merely present-day status and that which is likely to have continuing importance in the future.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Gnostic Syndrome: Anorexia Nervosa

Deirdre Barrett PHD and Harold Fine PHD

Harold Fine

Cambridge Mass. 02139 USA.

Harold Fine  

‘The Gnostic Syndrome’ discusses anorexia nervosa in terms of its poetics and in the context of a developmental object-relations theory. One case is used to illustrate the problems of therapy and the antecedents of the anorexia in childhood and adolescence. The paper forms an investigation into pre-moral states and the interaction of anorexia with character structure.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Vienna to Managua: Journey of a Psychoanalyst by Marie Langer. London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 paperback.

David Bell

David Bell

David Bell  

This is a quite remarkable book, one of those rare achievements that is at one and the same time a fascinating and intimate story of a person's life and an important intellectual contribution. Marie Langer, who died in 1987, lived a very full life, marked by personal integrity and deep political commitment. She was one of those few people who manage to be deeply immersed both in psychoanalysis and in the world of political and social events.

In the first part of the book she reflects in an honest and intimate way her early background in a bourgeois family in Vienna, tracing her early intellectual influences. She left Austria to join the International Brigade to fight against the fascists in the Spanish Civil War. She subsequently settled in Argentina, where she was a founder member of the Argentinian Psychoanalytic Association.
